title: Why Sustainable Aviation Fuel Is Slow to Reach Asian Hubs
dek: Carriers across the Gulf and Asia have committed to sustainable aviation fuel, but limited supply, high prices and thin local production keep SAF a small share of what jets actually burn.
body:
Sustainable aviation fuel, or SAF, remains the aviation industry's primary near-term lever for cutting carbon emissions, yet in 2025 it still accounts for only a small fraction of global jet-fuel consumption. For airlines and airports across the Gulf and Asia, including major hubs at Dubai, Doha, Singapore and across India, the central challenge is no longer ambition but supply: there simply is not enough certified SAF, and what exists costs substantially more than conventional kerosene.

The supply and cost gap

SAF is a drop-in fuel, meaning it can be blended with conventional jet fuel and burned in today's engines without modification. The constraint is production. Most current output comes from limited feedstocks such as used cooking oil and other wastes, and global volumes remain far below what would be needed to make a meaningful dent in aviation's emissions.

Because demand outstrips supply, prices stay high, and that premium is hard for carriers in price-sensitive markets to absorb. Mandates introduced in Europe and the United Kingdom are pulling volumes toward those regions, which can leave less for buyers elsewhere unless local production scales up.

Building Asian and Gulf capacity

The longer-term answer lies in expanding feedstock sources and production capacity closer to the region's hubs. Several Gulf and Asian governments and energy firms have signalled interest in domestic SAF and in newer pathways such as power-to-liquid fuels, though large-scale plants take years to build and finance.

  • SAF is a drop-in fuel but remains a small share of total jet-fuel use
  • High prices and limited feedstock constrain availability
  • European mandates are concentrating supply, pressuring other markets
  • Regional production capacity is the key to wider adoption

For now, SAF in Asia and the Gulf is best understood as a credible but constrained tool, one whose impact will grow only as production catches up with the commitments airlines have already made.
